articles.label: Analysis
articles.headline: Escambia County's $47 Million Budget Gap: Where the Cuts Will Land
articles.dek: County commissioners face a May 15 deadline to close a projected shortfall. Three departments are on the chopping block, and public safety isn't exempt.
brief: Escambia County faces a $47 million budget shortfall for FY 2027, driven by rising personnel costs and flat property tax revenues
Parks and Recreation, Public Works, and the Sheriff's Office administrative budget are targeted for cuts totaling $31 million
Commissioners must approve a preliminary budget by May 15 or trigger automatic across-the-board reductions
articles.body:
The spreadsheet on Commissioner Jeff Bergosh's desk runs fourteen pages. Red numbers dominate the right column. He flipped through it last Thursday afternoon in his Palafox Street office, pausing on page nine.

"This is where it gets ugly," he said.

Page nine details the Escambia County Sheriff's Office administrative budget—not deputies on patrol or jail operations, but the back-office functions that keep the agency running. Fleet maintenance. IT support. Training coordinators. The county's budget office has flagged $8.2 million in potential cuts there.

It's one piece of a $47 million puzzle the Escambia County Board of County Commissioners must solve by May 15.

How We Got Here

The shortfall didn't appear overnight. Three years of compounding pressures converged in the FY 2027 budget cycle.

Personnel costs rose 12% since 2024. Health insurance premiums jumped 18% last year alone. The county's pension obligations increased after the state legislature modified contribution requirements in 2025.

Revenue didn't keep pace. Property tax collections—the county's largest funding source—grew just 2.3% in the current fiscal year. The post-pandemic real estate surge that padded county coffers through 2024 has flattened. New construction permits dropped 31% year-over-year in the first quarter of 2026.

County Administrator Wes Moreno presented the shortfall projections to commissioners on April 3. The figure landed harder than expected.

"We anticipated a gap," Commissioner Steven Barry said at that meeting. "Not this gap."

Three Departments, Three Different Problems

The county budget office identified three departments for targeted reductions: Parks and Recreation, Public Works, and Sheriff's Office administration.

Parks and Recreation: $14.3 million in proposed cuts

The department operates 47 parks, three community centers, and the county's recreation programming. Under the proposed cuts, 23 part-time positions would be eliminated. Big Lagoon State Park's county-operated boat ramp would close. Programming at Bellview and Brownsville community centers would shift from daily to three days per week.

Parks Director Maria Santos told commissioners the reductions would effectively end youth sports leagues administered directly by the county. Those programs serve approximately 4,200 children annually.

"We'd have to partner with private leagues or let it go," Santos said at the April 17 budget workshop. "There's no middle option."

Public Works: $8.5 million in proposed cuts

Road resurfacing would drop from 47 miles annually to 29 miles. The department would defer replacement of 12 vehicles past their scheduled retirement dates. Brush collection cycles would extend from weekly to biweekly in unincorporated areas.

Public Works Director Tom Johnson warned that deferred maintenance compounds. Roads left untreated deteriorate faster. Vehicles run past useful life cost more to repair.

"You can cut this year," Johnson told commissioners. "But you'll pay double in three years."

Sheriff's Office Administration: $8.2 million in proposed cuts

Sheriff Chip Simmons submitted his proposed budget in February. It requested a 6% increase over current funding. The county's counterproposal cuts 4% from current levels.

The targeted reductions don't touch patrol staffing or jail operations—both protected by state mandates and consent decrees. Instead, they hit support functions: 11 civilian administrative positions, the agency's community outreach division, and capital equipment purchases including scheduled vehicle replacements.

Simmons has not publicly commented on the proposals. A spokesperson for the Sheriff's Office declined to make him available for an interview. In a written statement, the office said it "will work with county leadership to maintain public safety while respecting fiscal realities."

The Math That Doesn't Add

The three targeted departments account for $31 million in proposed cuts. That leaves $16 million unresolved.

Moreno's office has proposed covering the remainder through a combination of measures: drawing $7 million from reserve funds, implementing a countywide hiring freeze projected to save $4.5 million through attrition, and deferring $4.5 million in capital projects including the planned expansion of the Central Booking facility.

Commissioner Lumon May raised concerns about the reserve draw at the April 17 workshop.

"We're supposed to keep 15% in reserves," May said. "This puts us at 11%. What happens next year when we're in the same position with less cushion?"

Moreno acknowledged the risk. "It's a bridge," he said. "Not a solution."

What the Deadline Means

Florida law requires counties to adopt preliminary budgets before beginning the formal millage rate-setting process. Escambia County's internal deadline is May 15. Miss it, and the county charter triggers automatic across-the-board cuts of 4% to all departments—no targeting, no prioritization.

Commissioner Barry called that outcome "the worst of all options" at last week's meeting.

"You'd be cutting things that make no sense to cut while protecting things that maybe should be on the table," Barry said.

The board has two more workshops scheduled before the deadline: May 6 and May 13. Public comment will be accepted at both.

The Options Not on the Table

What commissioners haven't discussed publicly: revenue increases.

Escambia County's current millage rate sits at 7.85 mills, below the state-maximum 10 mills. A one-mill increase would generate approximately $28 million annually—enough to close more than half the gap.

No commissioner has proposed it. In interviews, three of the five cited election-year politics. All five seats face voters in November 2026.

"Raising taxes in an election year is a conversation no one wants to have," said one commissioner who requested anonymity to speak candidly. "So we're not having it."

Commissioner Doug Underhill, who has previously advocated for reduced county spending, said the shortfall validates his long-standing concerns about budget growth.

"We built a government we can't afford," Underhill said. "Now we're surprised we can't afford it."

What Comes Next

The May 6 workshop begins at 5:30 p.m. at the Ernie Lee Magaha Government Building, 221 Palafox Place. Moreno's office will present revised projections based on April revenue figures.

Three community groups have requested speaking time: the Escambia County Parks Foundation, the Pensacola Area Chamber of Commerce, and the local chapter of the Fraternal Order of Police.

The $47 million question remains open. Sixteen days remain to answer it.

articles.label: Investigation
articles.headline: The Stalled West Side: Why 12 Approved Projects Never Broke Ground
articles.dek: Developers blamed permits. The city blamed developers. Public records tell a different story about what's really holding up construction on Pensacola's west end.
brief: 12 development projects approved for Pensacola's west side between 2022 and 2024 have yet to break ground, representing over $180 million in stalled investment
Public records reveal a pattern of quiet extensions, ownership transfers, and financing contingencies that contradict public statements from both city officials and developers
Three projects have been sold to new ownership groups since approval, with original developers citing rising construction costs and insurance premiums as dealbreakers
articles.body:
The fence went up around the vacant lot at Cervantes and A Street in March 2023. A rendering appeared soon after: The Westgate Lofts, 84 units of workforce housing, completion date spring 2025.

Twenty-five months later, the fence is still there. The lot is still empty. The rendering has faded in the salt air.

Westgate Lofts is one of 12 development projects approved by Pensacola's planning authorities between January 2022 and December 2024 that have never broken ground. Together they represent more than $180 million in investment that exists only on paper—housing units that were supposed to ease a rental crisis, commercial space that was supposed to bring jobs, mixed-use developments that were supposed to transform the west side.

Ask the developers why, and they blame permitting delays. Ask the city, and officials point to developers who secured approvals but weren't ready to build.

Public records obtained by The Flightline through 14 separate requests over four months tell a more complicated story—one of quiet extensions granted without public notice, ownership transfers that reset project timelines, and financing contingencies that suggest some approvals were sought not to build but to shop.

The Pattern

Of the 12 stalled projects, seven received at least one administrative extension of their site plan approval. Under Pensacola's land development code, site plans expire after 24 months if construction hasn't begun. Extensions of up to 12 months can be granted by the development services director without returning to the planning board.

The city granted 11 such extensions across the seven projects. None were announced publicly. None appeared on planning board agendas. Residents who might have commented on the original approvals had no way of knowing the clock had been reset.

"That's standard practice," said Development Services Director Michael Sheridan in an interview. "Administrative extensions don't require public notice. The code is clear on that."

The code is clear. Whether that clarity serves the public interest is another question.

Three of the 12 projects have changed hands since approval. Westgate Lofts was sold in August 2024 to Coastal South Development Partners, a Georgia-based firm that has requested—and received—another 12-month extension. The original developer, Pensacola-based Riviera Holdings, cited "market conditions" in a brief statement at the time of the sale.

Those market conditions, according to financing documents included in the property transfer, came down to two numbers: construction costs 34% higher than 2022 projections and insurance premiums that tripled between approval and planned groundbreaking.

The Insurance Problem

Every developer The Flightline contacted mentioned insurance. None wanted to discuss it on the record.

One who agreed to speak anonymously—a principal in two of the stalled projects—described the math bluntly: "We approved a project in 2023 budgeting $180,000 annual for builder's risk and liability. By the time we were ready to pull permits in 2024, quotes came back at $540,000. That's not a cost increase. That's a different project."

Florida's property insurance crisis has been well documented at the residential level. Its impact on commercial and multifamily development has received less attention, but the stalled projects on Pensacola's west side suggest the effect is significant.

Of the 12 projects, nine include residential components. All nine are stalled. The three purely commercial projects—two retail centers and a medical office building—have also not broken ground, but two of those cite different issues: environmental remediation requirements discovered after approval and a dispute over utility easements.

What the City Knew

Emails obtained through public records requests show city staff were aware of financing struggles as early as mid-2023. A July 2023 email from a project manager in development services to Sheridan noted that "several approved projects are requesting pre-application meetings to discuss timeline concerns related to insurance and construction costs."

Sheridan's response, in full: "Let's discuss."

What was discussed, if anything, isn't documented in the records provided to The Flightline. The city cited deliberative process exemptions in withholding 23 additional emails from that period.

At a city council workshop in October 2023, council member Jennifer Brahier asked about the status of west side development projects. Then-Mayor D.C. Reeves responded that projects were "moving forward on their timelines."

By that date, according to records, three of the 12 had already missed their projected groundbreaking dates. Two had already requested extensions.

The Speculation Question

Among housing advocates, a darker theory circulates: that some developers never intended to build.

"You get an approval, you've created value," said Marcus Webb, director of the Pensacola Affordable Housing Coalition. "That approval is an asset. You can sell it. You can use it as collateral. You can hold the land and wait for conditions to change. Meanwhile, the housing we were promised doesn't exist."

There's no evidence of fraud in the public record. Developers are not required to break ground on approved projects. Extensions are available by administrative request. The system, as designed, allows for exactly what has happened.

But Webb argues the system needs examination. "When 12 projects representing thousands of housing units sit dormant while rents increase 40% in five years, that's not a permitting problem. That's a policy failure."

What Happens Next

Five of the 12 projects face final extension deadlines in 2026. If construction doesn't begin, the approvals will expire and the process would need to restart from scratch—new applications, new fees, new public hearings.

Coastal South Development Partners, the new owners of Westgate Lofts, told The Flightline they plan to break ground in the third quarter of 2026. They declined to discuss financing details.

Two other projects are reportedly under contract to new buyers, according to sources familiar with the transactions. Neither sale has closed.

The remaining projects exist in a kind of limbo: approved but not advancing, promised but not delivered, announced but not built.

Meanwhile, the fence at Cervantes and A Street continues to weather. The rendering continues to fade. And the 84 families who might have lived in workforce housing continue to look elsewhere.

The Flightline submitted detailed questions to all 12 project developers or their current ownership entities. Four responded. Two agreed to comment. A full accounting of each project, including approval dates, extension history, and current status, is available in our online document archive.

articles.label: Local News
articles.headline: Blue Angels Homecoming Week Brings 200,000 Visitors—and a Housing Crunch
articles.dek: With the team's return to Pensacola Beach for the annual air show, short-term rental prices have tripled and locals are cashing in or clearing out.
brief: Short-term rental rates on Pensacola Beach have surged to $800-$1,500 per night for the July 10-12 air show weekend, triple typical summer prices
Visit Pensacola projects 200,000 attendees over three days, generating an estimated $45 million in direct economic impact
Some year-round beach residents report being pressured by property managers to vacate during peak show dates
articles.body:
The sliding glass door of Unit 307 at the Emerald Dolphin condominiums faces south, toward the Gulf. On a Tuesday morning in late April, Linda Presswood stood on the balcony and watched two F/A-18 Super Hornets carve a practice arc over the water. The sound hit three seconds later. She flinched anyway.

"Every year," she said. "You'd think I'd be used to it."

Presswood, 64, has owned the two-bedroom unit since 2019. She rents it out maybe eight weeks a year, mostly to help cover the $847 monthly HOA fee. This July, for three nights during Blue Angels Homecoming, she'll collect $3,600.

"My property manager called in February," she said. "Told me she already had someone willing to pay $1,200 a night. I said book it."

The Annual Invasion

The Blue Angels Homecoming Air Show, scheduled this year for July 10-12, is Pensacola Beach's largest annual event. Visit Pensacola projects 200,000 attendees across the three days, with the Saturday show drawing the biggest crowds. The tourism agency estimates $45 million in direct spending—hotels, restaurants, fuel, parking, sunscreen.

For the short-term rental market, homecoming weekend has become something closer to a gold rush.

A Flightline review of Airbnb and VRBO listings found that beachfront properties typically renting for $250-$400 per night in early July were listed at $800-$1,500 for the July 10-12 dates. Many required three-night minimums. Most were already booked.

"It's supply and demand," said Marcus Chen, a vacation rental manager who oversees 34 properties on Pensacola Beach and in Gulf Breeze. "There's only so many beds. People plan this trip a year out."

Chen said 28 of his 34 properties were already reserved for the weekend. Average nightly rate: $1,100.

Locals Weigh Options

Not everyone sees the windfall the same way.

In the Portofino tower, where HOA rules permit short-term rentals, longtime residents describe an annual exodus. Some leave by choice. Others feel pushed.

Tom and Diane Kerrigan have owned a unit on the 14th floor since 2011. They don't rent it out. They live there. But every June, Diane said, the emails start.

"Our property management company sends these 'reminders' about the rental opportunity," she said. "Last year they offered to find us a hotel room in Milton if we'd agree to list it."

The Kerrigans declined. They spent the weekend at their daughter's house in Navarre instead.

"It's not worth the hassle," Tom Kerrigan said. "The noise, the crowds, the parking. We're too old for it."

Pensacola Beach residents who spoke to The Flightline described the homecoming period as both economically vital and personally exhausting. Traffic on Via de Luna backs up from the toll plaza to Casino Beach. Restaurant wait times stretch past two hours. The Publix on Pensacola Beach Boulevard runs out of ice by noon.

Hotel Rates Follow Suit

Traditional hotels show similar pricing patterns. The Hampton Inn on Pensacola Beach listed standard rooms at $489 per night for July 11, compared to $189 for the same room type the following weekend. The Margaritaville Beach Hotel showed weekend rates starting at $529.

Darryl Washington, general manager of the Holiday Inn Resort on Pensacola Beach, said his property has been fully booked for homecoming weekend since March.

"We could sell the rooms twice over," he said. "It's the single biggest weekend on our calendar."

Washington declined to share specific rate information but said the hotel adjusts pricing based on demand across all peak periods, including homecoming, the Foo Foo Festival, and Independence Day.

Economic Ripple Effects

The spending isn't confined to lodging. Restaurant owners along the beach strip describe homecoming as a make-or-break weekend.

Crabs We Got 'Em, the seafood spot at the foot of the Bob Sikes Bridge, added 12 temporary staff positions for the three-day stretch. Owner Bill Harmon said he expects to serve 3,000 customers on Saturday alone.

"We do three months of revenue in three days," Harmon said. "It's chaos, but it's the good kind."

Parking, typically $2 per hour in public lots, shifts to flat-rate pricing during the show. Santa Rosa County, which manages beach parking, will charge $40 per vehicle for all-day access in the Casino Beach lot during show hours.

A county spokesperson said the elevated rate helps manage demand and funds additional traffic control officers.

The View from the Air

The Blue Angels will perform their full demonstration on Saturday, July 11, with a practice show Friday and a shortened Sunday performance. The team, based at Naval Air Station Pensacola, considers the homecoming show its signature annual event.

Commander Scott Ind, the team's commanding officer, said at an April press event that homecoming carries particular weight for the squadron.

"This is where we live," he said. "This is where our families are. When we fly over that beach, we're flying over home."

For Presswood, watching from her balcony, the sentiment lands differently. She'll be in Birmingham that weekend, visiting her sister. Her renters—a family of five from Atlanta—will have the view to themselves.

"They seem nice," she said. "Sent me a very polite email asking about beach chair rentals."

She paused as another jet traced a line across the sky.

"$1,200 a night," she said. "That's the cost of watching from the best seat in the house."
